Authors: Abud G.; Alencar J.; Lavor C.; Liberti L.; Mucherino A.
Author full names: Abud, Germano (55871665800); Alencar, Jorge (55871289700); Lavor, Carlile (22135510100); Liberti, Leo (22135343900); Mucherino, Antonio (25621868300)
Title: An impossible combinatorial counting method in distance geometry
Affiliations: FAMAT, Federal University of Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil; IFTM, Federal Institute of Triângulo Mineiro, Brazil; IMECC, University of Campinas, Brazil; LIX CNRS, École Polytechnique, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Palaiseau, F-91128, France; IRISA and University of Rennes I, Rennes, France
Authors with affiliations: Abud G., FAMAT, Federal University of Uberlândia, Minas Gerais, Brazil; Alencar J., IFTM, Federal Institute of Triângulo Mineiro, Brazil; Lavor C., IMECC, University of Campinas, Brazil; Liberti L., LIX CNRS, École Polytechnique, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Palaiseau, F-91128, France; Mucherino A., IRISA and University of Rennes I, Rennes, France
Abstract: The Distance Geometry Problem asks for a geometric representation of a given weighted graph in RK so that vertices are points and edges are segments with lengths equal to the corresponding weights. Two problem variants are defined by a vertex order given as part of the input, which allows the use of a branching algorithm based on K-lateration: find two possible positions for the next vertex j using the positions of K predecessors and their distances to j, then explore each position recursively, pruning positions at need. Whereas the first variant only requires the K predecessors to exist, the second variant also requires them to be contiguous and immediately preceding j. For this variant, fixed-parameter tractability of the algorithm can be established by means of a solution counting method that only depends on the graph edges (rather than their weights). Only in the first variant, however, it is possible to efficiently construct a suitable vertex order directly from the graph. Since both fixed-parameter tractability and efficient vertex order construction are desirable properties, one would need an analogous solution counting method for the first variant. In this paper we prove that such a counting method cannot be devised for the first variant. © 2024 Elsevier B.V.
Author Keywords: Branch-and-Prune; DDGP; DGP; DMDGP; Solution symmetry
Index Keywords: Graph theory; Branch and prunes; DDGP; DGP; Distance geometry; DMDGP; Fixed-parameter tractability; Geometric representation; Geometry problems; Solution symmetry; Vertex ordering; Geometry

Authors: Lavor C.; Alves R.; Souza M.; José L.A.
Author full names: Lavor, Carlile (22135510100); Alves, Rafael (35726833000); Souza, Michael (56241180100); José, Luis Aragón (57326199500)
Title: NMR Protein Structure Calculation and Sphere Intersections
Affiliations: University of Campinas (IMECC-UNICAMP), SP, Campinas, 13081-970, Brazil; Federal University of ABC (CMCC-UFABC), SP, Sao Bernardo, 09606-070, Brazil; Federal University of Ceará (UFC), CE, Fortaleza, 60440-900, Brazil; Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Quéretaro, 76230, Mexico
Authors with affiliations: Lavor C., University of Campinas (IMECC-UNICAMP), SP, Campinas, 13081-970, Brazil; Alves R., Federal University of ABC (CMCC-UFABC), SP, Sao Bernardo, 09606-070, Brazil; Souza M., Federal University of Ceará (UFC), CE, Fortaleza, 60440-900, Brazil; José L.A., Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Quéretaro, 76230, Mexico
Abstract: Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) experiments can be used to calculate 3D protein structures and geometric properties of protein molecules allow us to solve the problem iteratively using a combinatorial method, called Branch-and-Prune (BP). The main step of BP algorithm is to intersect three spheres centered at the positions for atoms i - 3, i - 2, i - 1, with radii given by the atomic distances di-3,i, di-2,i, di-1,i, respectively, to obtain the position for atom i. Because of uncertainty in NMR data, some of the distances di-3,i should be represented as interval distances [di-3,i, di-3,i], where di-3,i ≤ di-3,i ≤ di-3,i. In the literature, an extension of the BP algorithm was proposed to deal with interval distances, where the idea is to sample values from [di-3,i, di-3,i]. We present a new method, based on conformal geometric algebra, to reduce the size of [di-3,i, di-3,i], before the sampling process. We also compare it with another approach proposed in the literature.  © 2020 Carlile Lavor et al., published by De Gruyter.
Author Keywords: Branch-and-Prune; Conformal Geometric Algebra; Molecular Distance Geometry Problem; NMR data

Authors: Alves R.; Lavor C.; Souza C.; Souza M.
Title: Clifford algebra and discretizable distance geometry
Source title: Mathematical Methods in the Applied Sciences
Affiliations: University of Campinas (IMECC-UNICAMP), Campinas, 13081-970, SP, Brazil; IFRJ, Nilópolis, 26530-060, RJ, Brazil; Federal University of Ceará (UFC), Fortaleza, 21945-970, CE, Brazil
Authors with affiliations: Alves R., University of Campinas (IMECC-UNICAMP), Campinas, 13081-970, SP, Brazil; Lavor C., University of Campinas (IMECC-UNICAMP), Campinas, 13081-970, SP, Brazil; Souza C., IFRJ, Nilópolis, 26530-060, RJ, Brazil; Souza M., Federal University of Ceará (UFC), Fortaleza, 21945-970, CE, Brazil
Abstract: Protein structure calculations using nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) experiments are one of the most important applications of distance geometry. The chemistry of proteins and the NMR data allow us to define an atomic order, such that the distances related to the pairs of atoms {i−3,i},{i−2,i},{i−1,i} are available, and solve the problem iteratively using a combinatorial method, called branch-and-prune. The main step of BP algorithm is to intersect three spheres centered at the positions for atoms i−3,i−2,i, with radius given by the atomic distances di−3,i,di−2,i,di−1,i, respectively, to obtain the position for atom i. Because of uncertainty in NMR data, some of the distances di−3,i may not be precise or even not be available. Using conformal Clifford algebra, in addition to take care of NMR uncertainties, which implies that we have to calculate sphere intersections considering that their centers and radius may not be fixed anymore, we consider a more flexible atomic order, where distances di−3,i are replaced by dj,i, where j⩽i−3. Authors: Santos C.; De Freitas R.; Salvatierra M.
Author full names: Santos, Clarice (57224778881); De Freitas, Rosiane (55827340200); Salvatierra, Mario (56023131900)
Title: Sphere intersection algorithms for Molecular Distance Geometry Problem
Source title: Proceedings of the 2013 39th Latin American Computing Conference, CLEI 2013
Affiliations: Programa de Pós-Graduação em Informática, Universidade Federai Do Amazonas, Manaus, Amazonas, Brazil; PPGI, Instituto de Computação, Universidade Federal Do Amazonas, Manaus - Amazonas, Brazil; Instituto de Computação, Universidade Federal Do Amazonas, Manaus - Amazonas, Brazil
Authors with affiliations: Santos C., Programa de Pós-Graduação em Informática, Universidade Federai Do Amazonas, Manaus, Amazonas, Brazil; De Freitas R., PPGI, Instituto de Computação, Universidade Federal Do Amazonas, Manaus - Amazonas, Brazil; Salvatierra M., Instituto de Computação, Universidade Federal Do Amazonas, Manaus - Amazonas, Brazil
Abstract: The problem of estimating the full three-dimensional structure of a molecule, determining the position in space of all the atoms that compose it, is called Molecular Distance Geometry Problem (MDGP). To do this from an incomplete set of distances is NP-hard computational problem, where to get a feasible solution in a reasonable execution time presenting interesting mathematical and computational challenges. In this work, continuous and discrete mathematical approaches to solve MDGP is revised, based on the analysis of two types of calculating of sphere intersection: solving nonlinear systems from interatomic Euclidean distance equations, or solving internal coordinate systems using matrix multiplication techniques. We adapted the Branch-and-Prune (BP) method considering four spheres intersection. Computational experiments using instances from PDB benchmark are performed, determining the 3D structure based on our theoretical assumptions in a competitive computational processing time. © 2013 IEEE.

Title: Erratum to [Cassioli et al., DAM197:27-41, 2015]: Finding repetition orders is in P (even if K=1)(Discrete Applied Mathematics, (2015), 197, C, (27-41), (S0166218X14003874), 10.1016/j.dam.2014.08.035)
Affiliations: LIX CNRS, Ecole Polytechnique, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Palaiseau, 91128, France
Authors with affiliations: Liberti L., LIX CNRS, Ecole Polytechnique, Institut Polytechnique de Paris, Palaiseau, 91128, France
Abstract: The Distance Geometry Problem (DGP) is as follows: given an integer K≥1 and a positively edge-weighted simple undirected graph G=(V,E,d) where d:E→R+, determine the existence of a realizationx:V→RK such that every edge {u,v}∈E is realized as a segment having length equal to duv[6]. One of the foremost applications of the DGP is the determination of the shape of proteins, where distances on the backbone are provided either by chemical information on covalent bonds, or by NOESY experiments [9]. In most cases, such distances yield instances (K,G) of the DGP where K=3 and G has a vertex order such that for all v∈V then v is adjacent to at least K predecessors whenever v>K: such graphs are called trilaterative. Under a genericity assumption, trilateration in RK with K adjacent predecessors u1,…,uK of v allows the computation of two possible positions for v. The Branch-and-Prune (BP) algorithm [4] exploits trilateration to generate a binary tree of possible partial realizations up to v; when v=n we have a realization of G. This exponential-time algorithm is made more efficient by pruning, which occurs whenever a new position xv for v is infeasible with respect to edges {u,v} where u∉{u1,…,uk}: if ‖xu−xv‖2≠duv then xv can be pruned. Therefore, by means of an appropriate vertex order, BP can find all incongruent realizations of G in RK. This makes the issue of finding such vertex orders very important. The problem of determining whether a given DGP instance (K,G) has such an order is called Trilateration Ordering Problem (TOP). If the order also ensures that the adjacent predecessors are contiguous, the problem is called Contiguous TOP (CTOP). We know that TOP is NP-hard in general but in P for fixed K, while CTOP is NP-hard even for fixed K[2]. CTOP orders, on the other hand, provide a theory of symmetry in the BP tree [8] that yields Fixed Parameter Tractability (FPT) [7], which may explain the computational success of BP on proteins. An analogous endeavour based on TOP orders is impossible [1]. To overcome the difficulties linked to finding these orders in the application of DGP to proteins, it was shown in [3] that it is possible to use a vertex sequence in which vertices appeared more than once. Such sequences were called repetition orders or re-orders, and the corresponding problem was called Repetition Order Problem (ReOP). We copy the definition of re-order from [2, Defn. 2.3]. A re-order is a sequence σ of m not necessarily distinct vertices from V such that: (1)the induced subgraph G[{v1,…,vK}] is a clique of size K(2)for all i∈{K+1,…,m} and j∈{i−K+1,…,i−1}, {vj,vi}∈E(3)for all i∈{K+1,…,m}, either {vi−K,vi}∈E or vi−K=vi(4)m≥|V|(5)m is bounded by a polynomial in |V|(6)for all v∈V we have v∈σ. the induced subgraph G[{v1,…,vK}] is a clique of size K for all i∈{K+1,…,m} and j∈{i−K+1,…,i−1}, {vj,vi}∈E for all i∈{K+1,…,m}, either {vi−K,vi}∈E or vi−K=vi m≥|V| m is bounded by a polynomial in |V| for all v∈V we have v∈σ. In [2, §3.3], it was claimed that because a re-order that never repeats vertices is a CTOP, then ReOP is NP-hard for any fixed K by inclusion of CTOP. This claim is wrong on two counts. First, ReOP was shown to be in P for K>1 in [5]: after this discovery, the claim on the NP-hardness of ReOP was reduced to hold only for K=1. Let us call this restricted re-order problem ReOP1: the claim then becomes that, for ReOP1, it is possible to set |σ|=m=|V|. With the constraint that m=|V|, the ReOP1 is exactly the same as CTOP with K=1, which is the same as Hamiltonian Path, known to be NP-complete. The error with this claim is that m is not part of the input but of the output: the re-order σ, which determines its length m, is indeed the certificate the existence of which is precisely the question asked by ReOP1. As a consequence, ReOP1 does not include CTOP even if K is fixed to 1. In this section, we prove that ReOP1 is in P. Proposition 1Given a DGP instance(K,G)whereK=1,Ghas a re-order iffGis connected. Given a DGP instance(K,G)whereK=1,Ghas a re-order iffGis connected. Proof⇒Assume G has a re-order σ. Then every vertex v∈V can be reachable from v1 via a walk over an orientation of a path in G compatible with σ. (⇐) Now assume that G is connected: consider a spanning tree T of G rooted at v1, and define σ as a Depth First Search (DFS) walk over T that includes the backtracking steps. We now show that σ is a 1-re-order by verifying every condition of the definition. Condition 1 is verified because G[{v1}] is a clique of size 1; condition 2 is verified because {i−K+1,…,i−1}=∅ for each i∈{2,…,m}, making it vacuous; condition 3 is verified because for each i∈{2,…,m} the construction of σ guarantees that {vi−1,vi}∈E; conditions 4 and 5 are verified since, again by construction, σ starts from v1 then passes over each edge of T exactly twice, so m=2(|V|−1)+1=2|V|−1 (a polynomial in |V|); condition 6 is verified because the tree T is spanning for G.□ Assume G has a re-order σ. Then every vertex v∈V can be reachable from v1 via a walk over an orientation of a path in G compatible with σ. (⇐) Now assume that G is connected: consider a spanning tree T of G rooted at v1, and define σ as a Depth First Search (DFS) walk over T that includes the backtracking steps. We now show that σ is a 1-re-order by verifying every condition of the definition. Condition 1 is verified because G[{v1}] is a clique of size 1; condition 2 is verified because {i−K+1,…,i−1}=∅ for each i∈{2,…,m}, making it vacuous; condition 3 is verified because for each i∈{2,…,m} the construction of σ guarantees that {vi−1,vi}∈E; conditions 4 and 5 are verified since, again by construction, σ starts from v1 then passes over each edge of T exactly twice, so m=2(|V|−1)+1=2|V|−1 (a polynomial in |V|); condition 6 is verified because the tree T is spanning for G.□ © 2026 Elsevier B.V.

Title: Constraint programming approaches for the discretizable molecular distance geometry problem
Affiliations: Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada
Authors with affiliations: MacNeil M., Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada; Bodur M., Department of Mechanical and Industrial Engineering, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada
Abstract: The Distance Geometry Problem (DGP) seeks to find positions for a set of points in geometric space when some distances between pairs of these points are known. The so-called discretization assumptions allow us to discretize the search space of DGP instances. In this paper, we focus on a key subclass of DGP, namely the Discretizable Molecular DGP, and study its associated graph vertex ordering problem, the Contiguous Trilateration Ordering Problem (CTOP), which helps solve DGP. We propose the first constraint programming formulations for CTOP, as well as a set of checks for proving infeasibility, domain reduction techniques, symmetry breaking constraints, and valid inequalities. Our computational results on random and pseudo-protein instances indicate that our formulations outperform the state-of-the-art integer programming formulations. © 2021 Wiley Periodicals LLC.
